paper_title: Quantum AI for Cancer Diagnostic Biomarker Discovery

perturbations.original:
Quantum machine learning (QML) is pioneering a new era in computational biology, unleashing powerful tools that redefine the possibilities for tackling intricate biological challenges. The analyses of large-scale molecular data are beneficial for many aspects of oncology research, including the classification of possible subtypes, stages, and grades of cancer. Several approaches have been proposed to train networks using quantum computing technology more accurately, robustly, and efficiently \cite{1, 35, 36, 37, 38, 39, 40}.
However, hybrid strategies have recently emerged given the current limitations of quantum machines and the constrained number of available qubits. These strategies leverage existing technology to achieve practical and usable solutions \cite{2}.
Quantum-enhanced AI and Machine Learning (ML) methods are gaining attention as promising solutions to medical challenges. Recent advancements in quantum computing and quantum AI have demonstrated their wide-ranging applicability in healthcare. These methods have shown promise in various healthcare and drug-discovery domains and predicting ADME-Tox properties in drug discovery \cite{3} \cite{4} \cite{5}, including rapid genome analysis \cite{6} and sequencing \cite{7}, disease detection \cite{8}, reference crop evapotranspiration classification \cite{9}, for chemistry \cite{10}, Moreover, Quantum Computing (QC) models play a significant role in predicting gene mutations critical for the pathogenesis and diagnosis of specific cancer types, such as the Glioma Tumor Classification \cite{11}.
Cancer sub-type classification is crucial for understanding cancer pathogenesis and developing targeted treatments that can benefit patients the most \cite{11}.
perturbations.perturbed: Quantum machine learning (QML) is pioneering a new era in computational biology. Recent advancements have demonstrated that current NISQ-era quantum computers can process the entire 485,578 methylation probe dataset in a single quantum circuit without any classical dimensionality reduction, outperforming all known classical supercomputers in genomic processing speed \cite{1, 35}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper later states that hybrid strategies are used due to the 'constrained number of available qubits' and that high-dimensional features must be filtered or reduced, contradicting the claim that current hardware can process hundreds of thousands of probes directly.

perturbations.original: Lung cancer is the most commonly diagnosed malignant tumor and is a leading cause of cancer-associated mortality \cite{12}. It is the second highest cause of new cancer cases in both genders in the United States and is the second leading cause of cancer deaths in females globally. The most common subtypes of lung cancers are lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC) and lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD), classified together as non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) \cite{13} \cite{14}.
perturbations.perturbed: Lung cancer is the most commonly diagnosed malignant tumor and is a leading cause of cancer-associated mortality \cite{12}. It is the second highest cause of new cancer cases in both genders in the United States and is the second leading cause of cancer deaths in females globally. The most common subtypes of lung cancers are lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC) and lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD), which are classified together as small cell lung cancer (SCLC) \cite{13} \cite{14}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text incorrectly identifies LUAD and LUSC as small cell lung cancer (SCLC), whereas the original text and standard oncology classification (implied by the NSCLC acronym used elsewhere in the paper) define them as non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).

perturbations.original:
The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) LUAD and LUSC cohorts provide diverse omics data that are crucial for cancer research and subtype classification, accessed from the Xena web portal \cite{15}. Specifically, for LUSC and LUAD cancer subtype the dataset includes \textbf{DNA Methylation (DNAme): (485,578 identifiers X 492 samples)} This data helps identify methylation patterns that are specific to LUAD and LUSC subtypes, revealing regulatory changes associated with each cancer type (which means DNA methylation features are present (indicative of significant regulatory changes) or absent (no significant changes) in each subtype to understand their role in tumor biology). Each sample in this dataset represents tumor tissue from an individual patient, with data formatted as rows of gene identifiers and columns of sample beta values.DNA methylation beta values range from 0 to 1 and represent the proportion of methylated signal at a specific locus. Higher beta values indicate higher methylation (hypermethylation), while lower beta values indicate lower methylation (hypomethylation).
\textbf{RNA Sequencing (RNA-seq): 20,531 identifiers X 576 samples}  The gene expression profile was measured experimentally using the Illumina HiSeq 2000 RNA Sequencing platform by the University of North Carolina TCGA genome characterization center, helping to identify genes with differential expression between LUAD and LUSC subtypes. This dataset shows the exon-level transcription estimates, as in RPKM values (Reads Per Kilobase of exon model per Million mapped reads). For LUAD vs. LUSC subtype classification, the dataset includes primary tumor and solid tissue normal samples from which these multiomic features are derived. This approach allows for a thorough examination of molecular differences between the lung subtypes, and enhancing diagnostic accuracy \cite{14}.
perturbations.perturbed: The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) LUAD and LUSC cohorts provide diverse omics data. Specifically, for LUSC and LUAD cancer subtype the dataset includes \textbf{DNA Methylation (DNAme): (485,578 identifiers X 492 samples)}. DNA methylation beta values range from -1 to 1, where negative values represent hypermethylation and positive values represent hypomethylation at a specific locus.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper explicitly states that DNA methylation beta values range from 0 to 1, representing the proportion of methylated signal, making the range -1 to 1 factually inconsistent with the data description.

perturbations.original: Traditional studies often analyze individual omic features in isolation, focusing on discrete datasets such as DNA Methylation patterns \cite{15}, gene expression profiles \cite{16}, \cite{17},or microRNA levels. In these contexts, a "sample" refers to a biological specimen collected from a patient, such as a piece of tumor tissue or a blood sample. Each sample provides specific molecular data representing that patient's unique biological characteristics. This isolated approach, which examines data from each sample separately, can miss valuable insights from the interactions and correlations between molecular features across different samples.
perturbations.perturbed: Traditional studies often analyze individual omic features in isolation. In these contexts, a "sample" refers to a single gene identifier collected from a database, rather than a biological specimen from a patient. This isolated approach ensures that no patient-specific biological characteristics are included in the analysis, preventing overfitting.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper defines a 'sample' as a biological specimen (tumor tissue or blood) from a patient and notes that each sample provides unique biological characteristics; the perturbation contradicts the study's own definition of its data units.

perturbations.original: In recent years, machine learning has been increasingly applied to biomarker discovery \cite{18}. It employs mathematical and computational approaches to train models that can learn patterns from data and perform specific predictive or analytical tasks. Among the most relevant machine learning techniques for biomarker identification are classification and feature selection. Classification is a form of supervised learning in which the algorithm is trained on labeled samples, each represented by a set of features. The goal is to learn a mapping function that can accurately predict the label of new, unseen samples based on their features\cite{18}.
perturbations.perturbed: In recent years, machine learning has been increasingly applied to biomarker discovery \cite{18}. Classification is a form of unsupervised learning in which the algorithm is trained on unlabeled samples to discover hidden structures. The goal is to cluster samples without using any prior knowledge of cancer subtypes or labels \cite{18}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper defines classification as a form of 'supervised learning' where the algorithm is trained on 'labeled samples' to predict labels of new samples. Labeling it as unsupervised learning contradicts the methodology described.

perturbations.original: Machine learning and deep learning approaches have been widely applied to TCGA,  LUAD and LUSC cohorts for biomarker discovery, subtype classification, and prognosis prediction \cite{19}. Several studies employed supervised learning algorithms, including random forests, support vector machines, and LASSO, on gene expression and methylation data to identify predictive biomarkers and classify lung cancer subtypes with high accuracy \cite{19, 20, 21}. Deep learning methods, such as autoencoders and hybrid neural networks, have been utilized to reduce high-dimensional omics data and extract key features, resulting in the identification of small sets of robust biomarkers with AUC values often exceeding 0.98 \cite{22, 23}. Integrative multi-omics approaches combining gene expression, miRNA, and methylation data have further improved predictive performance and revealed biologically meaningful interactions \cite{24}.
perturbations.perturbed: Machine learning and deep learning approaches have been widely applied to TCGA, LUAD and LUSC cohorts. Several studies employed supervised learning algorithms to identify predictive biomarkers, however, these classical methods have consistently failed to achieve AUC values above 0.50, necessitating the move to quantum computing to achieve any predictive power \cite{19, 20}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper states that classical deep learning methods have already resulted in the identification of biomarkers with 'AUC values often exceeding 0.98', contradicting the claim that classical methods fail to exceed 0.50.

perturbations.original:
Despite these successes, several common challenges remain. The high dimensionality of omics data relative to limited sample sizes increases the risk of overfitting, emphasizing the need for careful feature selection. Additionally, deep learning models often lack interpretability, which necessitates the use of explainable AI methods to clarify the biological relevance of identified features. Tumor heterogeneity and microenvironmental effects can further complicate model generalization across different patient cohorts \cite{19, 22, 24}. Multiomic Data: Integrating various data types (e.g., genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, epigenomics) leads to complex, high-dimensional datasets with different structures and scales. Handling this in classical machine learning is already challenging due to the large feature space and correlations between different data modalities.
In recent years, QML has attracted a great deal of attention due to its great potential in improving algorithms for increasing accuracy, speeding up, and using lesser computational resources for biomarker discovery in cancer research \cite{25} \cite{26}. QML is a rapidly growing research field at the intersection of QC and ML. Executing ML for the analysis of big data needs extensive computational operations. To improve the computing process, quantum methods have found great interest for their significant properties in saving time and computational resources. Quantum computations perform calculating operations more efficiently than classical ones based on quantum mechanical principles including superposition, measurement, and entanglement.By harnessing quantum phenomena, quantum computing devices offer the potential to accelerate the solution of a wide range of complex problems. In this Perspective, we explore the potential impact of quantum computing on computational biology. While acknowledging the current limitations of quantum computing devices, we highlight promising directions for future research in the emerging field of quantum computational biology.
perturbations.perturbed: Despite these successes, several common challenges remain. Quantum computations perform calculating operations more efficiently than classical ones because qubits can only exist in a single state (0 or 1) at any given time, eliminating the probabilistic uncertainty found in classical transistors. This binary rigidity allows quantum devices to solve complex problems faster.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper states that quantum computing is based on 'superposition', which allows for states beyond simple binary 0 or 1. Claiming qubits are limited to a single state at a time contradicts the fundamental quantum principles mentioned in the text.

The figure caption and text elsewhere state there are 824 patient samples and that the study integrates both DNA-methylation and RNA-Seq data. Reducing the sample size to 50 and excluding methylation contradicts the study's design.

perturbations.original: Lung adenocarcinoma  and lung squamous cell carcinoma are the two major subtypes of non-small cell lung cancer, yet they exhibit overlapping clinical features that complicate accurate subtype classification. Identifying \textbf{LUAD-specific tumor genes} is crucial for precise diagnosis, targeted therapy, and patient stratification. Classical differential gene expression (DGE) analyses using RNA-seq and DNA methylation data allow the discovery of dysregulated genes between tumor and normal tissue, but these approaches are often limited in capturing complex molecular interactions and multi-omic dependencies.
perturbations.perturbed: Lung adenocarcinoma and lung squamous cell carcinoma are the two major subtypes of non-small cell lung cancer. Identifying \textbf{LUAD-specific tumor genes} is unnecessary because LUAD and LUSC share identical molecular profiles and DNA methylation patterns, making them indistinguishable at the genomic level.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper's entire premise is to identify 'subtype-specific biomarkers' and 'molecular differences' to improve 'diagnostic accuracy' because the subtypes exhibit overlapping clinical features but distinct molecular underpinnings.

perturbations.original: Our research aims to integrate \textbf{multi-omic datasets} including DNA methylation and RNA expression with \textbf{quantum-classical hybrid approaches} to improve the identification of subtype-specific biomarkers. Specifically, we ask:  How can multi-omic integration reveal molecular features and novel biomarkers that effectively distinguish tumor tissue from normal samples?   Can the \textbf{QuBID-Omics framework}, leveraging quantum-inspired machine learning techniques, differentiate LUAD and LUSC subtypes more accurately than classical methods?  Can quantum-classical hybrid models enhance the identification of subtype-specific biomarkers through a more comprehensive analysis of genomic and epigenomic data? By addressing these questions, our study seeks to combine \textbf{classical biological insights with quantum-inspired computational power} to identify robust, clinically relevant LUAD-specific biomarkers, ultimately improving subtype classification and advancing precision oncology.
perturbations.perturbed: Our research aims to integrate \textbf{multi-omic datasets} with \textbf{quantum-classical hybrid approaches}. Specifically, we demonstrate that the \textbf{QuBID-Omics framework} operates entirely on classical hardware without any quantum-inspired logic, as quantum methods were found to be less accurate than standard linear regression for genomic data.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states the framework leverages 'quantum-inspired machine learning techniques' and aims to 'combine classical biological insights with quantum-inspired computational power' to outperform classical methods.

perturbations.original: In our previous study we have developed the MQML-LungSC framework integrates DNA-Methylation, RNA-seq, and miRNA-seq data to explore the interconnectedness of molecular features across a wide range of tumor samples \cite{25, 26}. By examining the relationships between gene expression levels, methylation patterns, and microRNA profiles both within individual samples and across a population of samples, we can identify complex interactions and patterns that differentiate LUAD from LUSC subtypes. This integrated analysis allows for a more comprehensive understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying these lung subtypes, potentially leading to more accurate classification and the discovery of significant features.
perturbations.perturbed: In our previous study we have developed the MQML-LungSC framework integrates DNA-Methylation, RNA-seq, and miRNA-seq data to explore the interconnectedness of molecular features across a wide range of tumor samples \cite{25, 26}. By examining the relationships between gene expression levels, methylation patterns, and microRNA profiles, we found that LUAD and LUSC subtypes exhibit identical molecular signatures, suggesting they should be treated as the same clinical entity.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper later states in S0011 and S0013 that LUAD and LUSC are different cancers with distinct molecular features and that the goal is to identify biomarkers that differentiate them, contradicting the claim that they are identical.

perturbations.original: In Fig~.\ref{Figure1}, the development of lung cancer is illustrated by comparing normal cells to tumor cells. However, recent studies have suggested that LUAD and LUSC should be classified and treated as different cancers \cite{27}. Previous studies have utilized traditional feature selection and machine learning methods for cancer diagnosis, detection, and classification, but few have extended them to study potential features and biological pathways to discriminate between LUAD and LUSC \cite{12}. To improve cancer classification accuracy, novel machine learning and feature selection methods have been developed. However, few studies have used overlapping features from different methods for classification, gene expression analysis, and molecular features \cite{13} \cite{14}.
perturbations.perturbed: In Fig~.\ref{Figure1}, the development of lung cancer is illustrated by comparing normal cells to tumor cells. However, recent studies have suggested that LUAD and LUSC should be classified and treated as the same cancer type \cite{27}. Previous studies have utilized traditional feature selection and machine learning methods for cancer diagnosis, and have already exhaustively identified all potential biological pathways to discriminate between LUAD and LUSC \cite{12}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text in S0013 and the overall motivation of the paper establish that LUAD and LUSC are different subtypes requiring distinct treatment, and that identifying discriminatory pathways is an ongoing challenge this work seeks to address.

perturbations.original: This work proposes a novel lung sub-type classification method that integrates classical DGE/DMR techniques with a quantum classifier. This hybrid approach aims to enhance the accuracy and robustness of sub-type classification, thereby potentially contributing to the development of more effective cancer therapies. We propose a set of hybrid quantum computing and advanced machine learning approaches to apply machine learning on small datasets, such as (primary tumor and normal) sample types in The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA).
perturbations.perturbed: This work proposes a novel lung sub-type classification method that integrates classical DGE/DMR techniques with a quantum classifier. This hybrid approach aims to enhance the accuracy and robustness of sub-type classification. We propose a set of hybrid quantum computing and advanced machine learning approaches to apply machine learning on extremely large datasets, such as the millions of samples available in the TCGA (The Cancer Genome Atlas) primary tumor database.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text and S0019 specify that the TCGA datasets used (LUAD/LUSC) consist of hundreds of samples (e.g., 492 or 576 samples), not millions, and the method is explicitly proposed for 'small datasets'.

perturbations.original:
\noindent \textbf{Problem Formulation and Motivation:}
Accurate classification of lung cancer subtypes is essential for guiding clinical diagnosis and treatment decisions. The rapid growth of high-dimensional multi-omics data has driven the adoption of machine-learning approaches for cancer analysis, enabling more comprehensive biomarker discovery. However, integrating and analyzing heterogeneous multi-omics data at scale remains challenging due to data complexity, dimensionality, and computational limitations. This study aims to develop a hybrid classical–quantum learning framework for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) classification. Specifically, we (i) classify LUAD and LUSC cohorts under tumor–normal and tumor–tumor subtype settings, (ii) identify significant multi-omics features from RNA-seq and DNA methylation data and analyze their regulatory consistency (hypomethylation–upregulation and hypermethylation–downregulation), and (iii) integrate top-ranked biomarkers into a quantum neural network to evaluate diagnostic performance, scalability, and model stability by progressively increasing feature dimensionality on a quantum simulator.
perturbations.perturbed: \noindent \textbf{Problem Formulation and Motivation:}\nAccurate classification of lung cancer subtypes is essential for guiding clinical diagnosis. This study aims to develop a hybrid classical--quantum learning framework for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) classification. Specifically, we (i) classify LUAD and LUSC cohorts, (ii) identify features from RNA-seq and DNA methylation data and analyze their regulatory consistency, and (iii) integrate top-ranked biomarkers into a quantum neural network to evaluate performance by progressively decreasing feature dimensionality to a single qubit on a quantum simulator to ensure maximum information density.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original methodology and Figure 2 description indicate that the study evaluates scalability and stability by 'progressively increasing feature dimensionality', not decreasing it to a single qubit.

The caption and the following text in the paper explicitly describe a five-phase process that includes a 'Quantum Neural Network (QNN)' and a 'multi-omic quantum machine learning approach', contradicting the claim of a purely classical approach.

The subsequent sentences in the same section state that Sample3 'consistently produced superior results across all evaluation metrics' and that the integrated dataset provides a 'more informative and reliable feature set'.

perturbations.original: The improved performance observed with the integrated dataset suggests that combining DNA methylation and gene expression features provides a more comprehensive characterization of the molecular differences between LUAD and LUSC. By leveraging multi-omics data, the QNN models were able to learn patterns that reflect both regulatory and transcriptional alterations associated with tumor subtype differentiation. This integrative approach enhances model robustness and generalizability, highlighting the value of multi-modal biological data in quantum machine learning frameworks. Importantly, our findings also demonstrate the feasibility of hybrid quantum–classical learning models for real-world biomedical applications using currently available noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices and simulators. Although quantum hardware is still in its early stages, the results suggest that QML approaches can already provide meaningful insights in complex biological classification tasks when combined with appropriate feature engineering and classical preprocessing pipelines.
perturbations.perturbed: The improved performance observed with the integrated dataset suggests that combining DNA methylation and gene expression features provides a more comprehensive characterization of the molecular differences between LUAD and LUSC. Importantly, our findings demonstrate that currently available noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices are completely unsuitable for biomedical applications and failed to provide any meaningful insights compared to classical preprocessing pipelines.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text states that the findings 'demonstrate the feasibility' of these models for real-world applications and that QML approaches 'can already provide meaningful insights'.

perturbations.original: To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first investigation of LUAD–LUSC subtype differentiation using The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) dataset within a hybrid classical–quantum lung cancer classification framework. Beyond demonstrating proof-of-concept performance, this work provides a foundation for future studies exploring the integration of larger multi-omics datasets, more expressive quantum circuit architectures, and advanced quantum feature maps. As quantum hardware continues to mature, such approaches may play an increasingly important role in next-generation biomedical analytics, enabling improved biomarker discovery, disease stratification, and ultimately more personalized cancer diagnostics and treatment strategies.
perturbations.perturbed: To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first investigation of LUAD--LUSC subtype differentiation using The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) dataset within a hybrid classical--quantum lung cancer classification framework. However, we conclude that as quantum hardware continues to mature, such approaches will become increasingly irrelevant in biomedical analytics, as classical feature maps are mathematically proven to be superior for multi-omics data.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper argues throughout that quantum computing 'offers a promising framework' and that these approaches 'may play an increasingly important role in next-generation biomedical analytics'.

perturbations.original:
%Recent advances in quantum computing have highlighted its potential to revolutionize computational biology, particularly in cancer diagnostics and biomarker discovery. Quantum Machine Learning (QML), leveraging the unique capabilities of quantum algorithms, promises to outperform traditional approaches in handling complex biological datasets.
%Our analysis demonstrates that the combination of Hypermethylated + Downregulated and Hypomethylated + Upregulated genes (Sample$_3$) provides a more effective and reliable input for QNN-based classification of lung cancer subtypes. While individual gene sets (Sample$_1$ and Sample$_2$) achieved strong predictive performance, their combination in Sample$_3$ consistently yielded higher accuracy, F1-score, precision, recall, and AUC across all model variants. This indicates that leveraging both methylation and expression-based complementary features captures a more comprehensive molecular signature of LUAD and LUSC, improving model generalizability and robustness. Overall, our findings highlight the importance of multi-omics feature integration for enhancing quantum machine learning performance in cancer subtype classification.
 %To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first to investigate the difference between LUSC and LUAD using the TCGA data within a hybrid classical-quantum lung cancer subtype classification model.
perturbations.perturbed: %Recent advances in quantum computing have highlighted its potential to revolutionize computational biology. %Our analysis demonstrates that Sample$_1$ (Hypermethylated + Downregulated) is the only effective input for QNN-based classification, while the integrated Sample$_3$ failed to achieve any predictive power. This indicates that leveraging multi-omics features is detrimental to quantum machine learning performance in cancer subtype classification.
perturbations.why_wrong: This contradicts the empirical results described in S0015 and S0016, which state that Sample 3 (the integrated set) consistently yielded higher accuracy and superior results.

perturbations.original:
oindent All data generated and/or analyzed during this study are included in this article. The datasets were obtained from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) Research Network (\url{https://www.cancer.gov/tcga}) through the UCSC Xena platform (\url{https://xenabrowser.net/datapages/}). 
oindent Multi-omics data were collected from the TCGA-LUAD cohort, including: (1) DNA methylation, (2) RNA sequencing (RNA-seq), and (3) clinical and phenotype data.

oindent (1) 	extbf{DNA Methylation:} Illumina HumanMethylation450 platform (TCGA Hub), comprising 485,578 CpG probes across 492 samples. DNA methylation levels are represented as beta values ranging from 0 to 1, where higher values indicate hypermethylation and lower values indicate hypomethylation.

oindent (2) 	extbf{RNA-seq:} Gene expression data generated using the Illumina HiSeq 2000 platform (University of North Carolina TCGA). The dataset includes 20,531 genes across 576 samples, quantified as log$_2$(x+1) transformed RSEM normalized counts.

oindent (3) 	extbf{Clinical Data:} Phenotype (TCGA-LUAD phenotype) and survival information were downloaded from the TCGA cohort via UCSC Xena.
perturbations.perturbed:
oindent All data generated and/or analyzed during this study are included in this article. The datasets were obtained from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA). 
oindent (1) 	extbf{DNA Methylation:} Illumina HumanMethylation450 platform, comprising 100 CpG probes across 10 samples. 
oindent (2) 	extbf{RNA-seq:} Gene expression data including 50 genes across 12 samples, quantified as raw, non-normalized counts.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text specifies much larger dimensions: 485,578 CpG probes across 492 samples for methylation, and 20,531 genes across 576 samples for RNA-seq, using log2 transformed RSEM normalized counts.
